identity: Methylcobalamin; synonyms mecobalamin, MeCbl, MeB12, methyl-B12. The methylated bioactive cobalamin coenzyme form. CAS 13422-55-4. Molecular formula C63H91CoN13O14P, MW ~1344.4 g/mol. Dark red crystals forming cherry-red transparent aqueous solutions. Related vitamers: cyanocobalamin (CAS 68-19-9, the synthetic, more shelf-stable form used as standard feed B12), hydroxocobalamin, adenosylcobalamin. In this formula it appears as "Methyl-B12 5000mcg" in S3 Reset B (per 120 mL), S4 Oxygen NUSAVA B-complex (5 mL/250 mL), and F5 ATP Express (5000 mcg/150 mL). (Tier 1 identity: Wikipedia/PubChem corroborated MW+CAS+formula; Tier 2 datasheet.)
r1_solubility: Intended in-solution concentration is extremely low: 5000 mcg (5 mg) per 120 mL in S3 = ~0.042 mg/mL; per 150 mL in F5 = ~0.033 mg/mL; after 1:3 use-dilution ~0.008-0.010 mg/mL. Methylcobalamin is freely soluble in water ("freely form cherry-colored transparent solutions in water", Wikipedia/PubChem Tier 2; the close analog cyanocobalamin has documented aqueous solubility ~12.5 mg/mL = 1/80 g/mL, DrugBank/monograph Tier 1-2). Headroom = ~12500 mcg/mL solubility / ~42 mcg/mL intended = ~300x. Solubility is NEVER limiting for B12 at vitamin micro-doses. Verdict R1: OK (massive headroom, >300x). EXACT methylcobalamin mg/mL aqueous solubility from a pharmacopeial monograph is UNKNOWN (only the cyanocobalamin 12.5 mg/mL figure and qualitative "freely soluble" for MeCbl are sourced), but this gap is immaterial given the 300x+ margin.
r2_stability: Dominant degradation pathway in water is PHOTOLYSIS, and methylcobalamin is the LEAST photostable common vitamer. Aerobic aqueous photolysis (pH 1-12) yields aquocobalamin in acid and hydroxocobalamin in alkali; apparent first-order photolysis rate constants range 2.80 (pH 12) to 27.25x10^-2 min^-1, with the MAXIMUM photolysis rate at pH ~5.0 (Ahmad et al., J Photochem Photobiol A/Sci Direct S1010603018302545, Tier 1). RP-HPLC stability-indicating study (Amer 2022, PMID 35670513, Tier 1): photodegradation followed zero-order kinetics with half-life ~0.99 h at 1971.53 lux; most stable at pH 5, least stable at pH 2; pronounced susceptibility to acidic, alkaline AND photolytic stress. Comparative photodegradation: MeCbl lost ~39% vs cyanocobalamin ~4-7% after 60 min UV (cyanocobalamin/hydroxocobalamin are the most stable; RJPT/PMID 16414723, Tier 1-2). Light-protected stability: extemporaneous MeCbl injections stable >=181 days at room temp ONLY in amber vials protected from light (PMID 31085783, Tier 1). CRITICAL CONFLICT WITH THIS FORMULA: the liquids sit at pH 5.8-6.2, i.e. squarely in the pH ~5 photolysis-maximum window, and are pigmented translucent solutions with no guaranteed opacity -> worst-case photolytic loss of the active coenzyme over realistic handling/storage. Thermal/oxidative loss compounds this. R2 verdict for any LIQUID at this pH: BLOCK / loss-prone active. Mitigation in water: amber/opaque packaging, cold/dark storage, antioxidant; sorbitol reportedly stabilizes B12 vitamers (Hogrefe IJVNR a000578, Tier 2). Cleaner fix: remove from water entirely (dry preload), where even photolytic loss is sharply reduced and shelf-life is ~12-18 mo.
r3_conflicts: Pairwise conflicts with co-formulated ingredients: (1) Methylcobalamin + sodium ASCORBATE / ascorbic acid (S2 6g ascorbate; vitamin-C-containing B-complex): ascorbate accelerates cobalamin destruction (well-documented vitamin C -> B12 degradation; cobalamins are redox-labile) -> avoid co-solution; mitigation: separate, keep B12 dry. Pair -> redox/ascorbate-driven degradation -> separate. (2) Methylcobalamin + trace transition metals (Fe/Cu contaminants, and the Mg/electrolyte load): metal ions catalyze cobalamin photo-oxidation and free-radical degradation -> mitigation: chelator/sequestrant, low-metal water, dry storage. (3) Methylcobalamin + other photolabile/redox actives (P5P/B6 100 mg in S3, NAC thiol): thiols (NAC) can reductively cleave the Co-C bond and reduce cobalt; B12 + thiol in solution = mutual instability -> mitigation: never co-dissolve B12 with NAC (another reason both go dry). (4) Light + near-neutral pH (formula 5.8-6.2): not an ingredient but the matrix itself maximizes photolysis. No precipitation/chelation hard-stop with citrate (B12 stays in solution), so the issue is degradation, not insolubility. Pair -> mechanism -> mitigation all point to the same fix: B12 belongs in a dry, opaque, metal-/ascorbate-/thiol-free premix.
avian_evidence: Poultry B12 REQUIREMENT: ~20 mcg/kg feed meets growing chicken/duck requirement (Halle & Henning broiler/Pekin duck study; NRC Nutrient Requirements of Poultry 1994 lists ~3-10 mcg/kg for chickens; typical commercial supplementation 5-20 mcg/kg feed). B12 is an ESSENTIAL vitamin for poultry — birds cannot synthesize adequate B12 (gut synthesis insufficient; cobalt-dependent), so dietary supplementation is the standard route (Merck Vet Manual, Tier 1; MDPI Animals 2019 PMC6770686 Pekin-duck cyanocobalamin growth study, Tier 1). MECHANISM: methionine synthase / methylmalonyl-CoA mutase cofactor — methyl-group metabolism, supports the betaine/methionine methylation pathway already targeted by the formula's betaine. ACUTE vs CHRONIC: B12 is a slow-turnover, hepatically-stored vitamin repleted over days/weeks — it is a CHRONIC status nutrient, NOT an acute intra-fight ergogenic; a between-rounds sip delivers no acute benefit. MAX TOLERABLE: B12 is among the LEAST toxic vitamins; NRC Vitamin Tolerance of Animals lists no defined upper neurotoxic limit (water-soluble, uricotelic renal clearance of excess). FORM: for dry feed repletion, CYANOCOBALAMIN is the standard, more photostable, cheaper, and adequate form — the methyl form is not specifically required for status repletion. DOSE in formula: 5000 mcg/serving is ~250-1000x the daily poultry requirement — pharmacologic and wasted via renal clearance, plus the costly methyl form is the one most degraded by the wet matrix. No avian-specific evidence supports methyl-B12 over cyano, nor an acute liquid B12 ergogenic effect.
verdict: SKIP methylcobalamin in ALL liquids (S3, S4 B-complex, F5, and any revised liquid drink) — BLOCK in the wet matrix because the formula pH 5.8-6.2 coincides with the photolysis-maximum (~pH 5) for the least-photostable vitamer, and it co-resides with ascorbate/NAC/metals that degrade it. ADD-conditional to the DRY PRELOAD only, preferably substituting CYANOCOBALAMIN (more photostable, cheaper, adequate for poultry status). Recommended dose: dry feed premix targeting ~20-50 mcg B12/bird/day (a modest multiple of the ~20 mcg/kg-feed requirement; bird eats ~120-150 g/day), as cyanocobalamin in an opaque/foil, desiccated micro-premix with 2-step geometric dilution for uniformity. Net: delete the 5000 mcg liquid B12 line items; consolidate B12 to one dry source. This removes a BLOCK-severity stability defect with zero loss of avian benefit (B12 is chronic-status, not acute).
cautions: Welfare/producibility notes (non-blocking): (1) 5000 mcg/serving is pharmacologic overkill vs ~20 mcg/kg-feed poultry requirement — not a toxicity hazard (B12 essentially non-toxic, renally cleared in uricotelic birds) but a cost/labeling waste and signals dose-by-guess formulation. (2) The methyl form is a deliberate cost-and-stability disadvantage vs cyanocobalamin; for dry feed status repletion cyano is standard and adequate — confirm no specific methyl-form requirement before substituting (there is none for status). (3) If any legacy B12 liquid is retained, it MUST be amber/opaque, cold/dark stored, kept away from ascorbate/NAC/trace metals, and made fresh; even then realistic photolytic loss makes label claim unreliable. (4) Methyl-B12 marketing ("active form", "energy") is Tier-3 hype with no avian acute-performance evidence; do not let it justify the liquid line items.
sources: Amer SM et al. Stability-indicating RP-HPLC method for methylcobalamin... photodegradation kinetics and pH rate profiling. J Sep Sci 2022. PMID 35670513 / DOI 10.1002/jssc.202200132 (Tier 1) — zero-order photodegradation, t1/2 ~0.99 h @1971.53 lux; most stable pH 5, least stable pH 2; acidic/alkaline/photolytic susceptibility

Ahmad I et al. Photolysis of methylcobalamin in aqueous solution: a kinetic study. J Photochem Photobiol A 2018; ScienceDirect S1010603018302545 (Tier 1) — photolysis rate max at pH ~5.0; k 2.80-27.25x10^-2 min^-1; aquo/hydroxocobalamin products
Extemporaneously prepared methylcobalamin injections: stability with/without preservative and light exposure. PMID 31085783 (Tier 1) — stable >=181 d RT only in amber, light-protected

Photodegradation Effects of Cyanocobalamin, RJPT 2025 18(2)-65; and PMID 16414723 stability of cyanocobalamin in sunlight/artificial light (Tier 1-2) — MeCbl ~39% vs cyano ~4-7% loss at 60 min UV; cyano/hydroxo most stable
Wikipedia/PubChem Methylcobalamin: CAS 13422-55-4, MW ~1344.4, C63H91CoN13O14P, freely soluble cherry-red aqueous solutions (Tier 2 identity/qualitative solubility)
DrugBank/monograph cyanocobalamin aqueous solubility ~12.5 mg/mL (1/80 g/mL) (Tier 1-2 analog solubility)
Halle I, Henning M. Influence of vitamin B12 and cobalt on growth of broiler chickens and Pekin ducks (Tier 2) — ~20 mcg B12/kg feed meets requirement
NRC Nutrient Requirements of Poultry (1994) + NRC Vitamin Tolerance of Animals (Tier 1) — chicken B12 req ~3-10 mcg/kg; no defined upper toxic limit
Merck Veterinary Manual, Nutritional Requirements of Poultry (Tier 1) — B12 essential, dietary supplementation required
Pekin duck supplemental cyanocobalamin growth/hematology study, MDPI Animals 2019, PMC6770686 (Tier 1) — cyanocobalamin used as standard supplemental form
Sorbitol enhances physicochemical stability of B12 vitamers, Int J Vitam Nutr Res, DOI 10.1024/0300-9831/a000578 (Tier 2) — excipient stabilization
blocking_gaps: UNKNOWN: exact pharmacopeial aqueous solubility of methylcobalamin in mg/mL @25C — only qualitative 'freely soluble' (MeCbl) and the cyanocobalamin analog 12.5 mg/mL are sourced; immaterial here given >300x headroom at vitamin micro-dose

UNKNOWN: quantitative photolytic loss rate specifically at pH 5.8-6.2 in THIS sugar/citrate/ascorbate matrix (sourced data brackets pH 5 max and general aqueous behavior; matrix-specific kinetics not measured)

UNKNOWN: gamefowl-specific (not broiler/duck) B12 requirement and any methyl-vs-cyano efficacy difference in birds — no avian study isolates the methyl form; treated as equivalent-to-cyano for status repletion

corrections: Citation-attribution error (not a fabrication): the comparative figure 'MeCbl ~39% vs cyanocobalamin ~4-7% loss after 60 min UV' is attributed to PMID 16414723 / RJPT. PMID 16414723 (Ahmad et al., 'Stability of cyanocobalamin solutions in sunlight and artificial light') is a CYANOCOBALAMIN-ONLY study (950 and 95 ug/mL at pH 4.0/5.5/7.0; sunlight loss 14.7-21% at 950 ug/mL, 56-81% at 95 ug/mL), NOT a head-to-head MeCbl-vs-cyano comparison. The MeCbl 39% comparative figure actually comes from the Chamle/Shane 'Photodegradation of Methylcobalamin and Its Determination in a Commercial Formulation' (IJPS) paper, which the dossier did not cite by name. Fix: attribute the 39% MeCbl figure to the IJPS Chamle/Shane paper and re-label PMID 16414723 as cyanocobalamin-only photodegradation evidence. The DIRECTIONAL claim (MeCbl is less photostable than cyano/hydroxo) remains correct.

Imprecise wording: dossier states formula pH 5.8-6.2 'coincides with the photolysis-maximum (~pH 5).' Ahmad's verified bell-shaped k-pH profile peaks at pH ~5.0; at pH 5.8-6.2 the rate is just past the apex but still near-maximal. More accurate: 'sits on the high-rate shoulder just past the pH~5 photolysis maximum,' not 'coincides with.' Does not change the R2 BLOCK verdict.

Solubility caveat is correctly disclosed but worth restating: the 12.5 mg/mL figure is cyanocobalamin's saturation solubility (1:80 water, confirmed WHO/JP monograph), used as an ANALOG. Exact pharmacopeial MeCbl mg/mL is genuinely UNKNOWN. The R1 OK / >300x-headroom conclusion is still valid because intended dose is a vitamin micro-dose (~0.042 mg/mL) far below even the analog's saturation.
_verify.notes: All load-bearing PMIDs/DOIs are REAL and say what is claimed; spot-checked 7 sources via WebSearch. PMID 35670513 (Amer 2022, J Sep Sci, DOI 10.1002/jssc.202200132): confirmed zero-order photodeg t1/2 ~0.99 h @1971.53 lux, most stable pH 5, least pH 2. Ahmad ScienceDirect S1010603018302545: confirmed bell-shaped k-pH, MAX photolysis at pH ~5.0, k 2.80->27.25x10^-2 min^-1. PMID 31085783: confirmed 181-d stability only amber/light-protected. Ascorbate->B12 destruction (PMID 4479087, AAPS PMC4179674) REAL. Thiol reductive Co-C cleavage (PMID 1276183, 17567060) REAL. Cyanocobalamin 1:80 water=12.5 mg/mL confirmed. NRC poultry B12 ~3-20 mcg/kg + cyano-as-standard confirmed. NO fact inversions: R1 OK (300x headroom) correct; R2 BLOCK-in-liquid logic (least-photostable vitamer on the pH~5 high-rate shoulder, co-residing with ascorbate/NAC/metals) sound; R3 ascorbate/thiol/metal conflicts all real with correct mechanisms. Avian frame correct and NOT inverted: B12 essential for poultry (gut synthesis insufficient), CHRONIC hepatic-storage status nutrient not acute ergogenic, ~20 mcg/kg-feed requirement, 5000 mcg/serving = ~250-1000x req = pharmacologic overkill but non-toxic (renal clearance, uricotelic), cyanocobalamin correctly recommended for dry preload over the more-labile methyl form. Verdict SKIP-in-all-liquids / ADD-conditional-to-dry-cyano is well-justified. Only defects are a source-attribution slip and one over-precise pH phrasing -- neither alters the conclusion. CONFIRMED.
